mnemonics.title: A* Formula Remember
mnemonics.content: F = G + H: Think 'F = Going Home' - G is Gone (cost from start), H is Home (estimated cost to goal)

mnemonics.title: Admissible Heuristic
mnemonics.content: A = Always underestimate. 'Admissible = Always Less Than Or Equal' to the true cost.

mnemonics.title: Consistent Heuristic Triangle
mnemonics.content: Consistent = Can't Cheat: h(n) ≤ cost + h(n'). You can't get to goal cheaper than going through a neighbor first.

acronyms.usage: Most famous informed search algorithm using f(n)=g(n)+h(n)
Memory-efficient version of A* using iterative deepening with f-cost bounds
A* variant that limits memory usage by dropping worst nodes

acronyms.expansion: f of n = g of n + h of n
acronyms.usage: Evaluation function in A* combining actual and estimated costs
Estimated cost from node n to goal state

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a scoreboard: g(n) is distance traveled (fuel used), h(n) is distance remaining (gas stations ahead). f(n) is total journey estimate.
Admissible = floor estimate (never too high). Consistent = triangle where each step forward costs something, never negative.
Think of heights: if h1 is always taller than h2, h1 dominates. Better coverage means less searching.
Greedy = only looks at destination sign (h). A* = looks at both odometer (g) AND destination sign (h).

analogies.analogy: Like GPS navigation: h(n) is the 'as-the-crow-flies' straight-line distance to destination, while actual path cost g(n) is the road distance traveled.
Like driving with both odometer and map: g(n) shows how far you've driven, h(n) shows estimated distance to goal, f(n) is total estimated journey.
Like an optimistic friend who never overestimates travel time - they might underestimate but never promise arrival too early.
Like a trustworthy map: the distance from A to C is never greater than distance A to B plus B to C.
Between two weather predictions, the one with higher accuracy (closer to actual) dominates - it gives you more useful information.
